Two weeks of procurement analysis. One question we haven't answered.

The preferred supplier program is intact. The contract is honored. The compliance checks are passing.

And yet: the recruiters who earned that status are gone, the budget is 23% larger than any system reported, and the hiring manager who needed the role filled last week chose a supplier from her personal network.

Every governance mechanism is functioning exactly as designed.

Is that the problem?

What would it mean to redesign workforce governance not to better enforce what has already been decided, but to guide what is about to be decided?

We explore that next week.

#ServicesProcurement #WorkforceSuperIntelligence #SpendGovernance #Prosperix

Well before he was leading the Rebellion and clashing lightsabers with Darth Vader, Luke Skywalker was a mere apprentice, training in the swamps of Dagobah while carrying his master Yoda on his back.  

The experience of apprentices and trainees in the real world is not that different from Luke’s. Take, for example, the junior lawyer drowning under a pile of paperwork, or the corporate intern taking coffee orders, or a line of aspiring chefs shucking oysters all day. As grueling as it might seem, this scut work is often viewed as a necessary evil because it’s the only way many apprentices are able to compensate their masters for training them. 

“The master might say, ‘Okay, I’m going to train you. But in return, I’m going to have you working for me for a while, and I’m going to pay you less than how much you produce,’” says Luis Rayo, a professor of strategy at the Kellogg School and an expert on contract theory and organizational economics.   

But the recent rise of AI has complicated this long-standing dynamic. Large-language models like Claude and Gemini are performing many common apprentice tasks increasingly well, often with much greater efficiency than the average trainee. Indeed, recent research already shows a decline in hiring for entry-level, but not experienced, workers in the occupations most exposed to generative AI.  

“Apprenticeship-like systems are a very common way in which economies solve the difficult problem of transferring human capital from one generation to the next,” Rayo says. “AI risks destroying that system, and the problem then is, who’s going to train the next generation of experts?” 

To better understand the possibility of that happening, Rayo collaborated with Luis Garicano of the London School of Economics to mathematically model what happens when AI enters the apprentice–master relationship.  

They found that the more tasks AI takes over from the apprentice, the less viable apprenticeships become. Yet they also found that AI could help elevate the peak ability and productivity of advanced-level apprentices, which could increase the value of apprentices and give masters more incentive to hire one.  

“So it’s a race between the two [effects of AI],” Rayo says. “One shrinks profits, the other one grows profits, and whichever one wins determines whether an apprenticeship is profitable.”  

Read more about the research in "Are Apprentices an Endangered Species?" at Kellogg Insight. https://lnkd.in/g7zH9y8v #KelloggLeader #Strategy #Internships #Apprenticeship #AI (Art by Veronica M.)

Everyone is building data pipelines. Palantir just made the pipeline itself obsolete.
I know that's a spicy take. Let me explain.
For years, our job as data engineers was to move data from Point A to Point B — clean it, transform it, load it, repeat.

Palantir's AIP doesn't just sit on top of your data. 
It builds an Ontology — a living, semantic knowledge graph that understands:
what your data means, 
how objects relate, and 
why a decision matters in business context.
That's a fundamentally different architecture.

Instead of: Raw data → ETL → Warehouse → Dashboard → Human decision

AIP does: Raw data → Ontology → AI agents → Autonomous action

The scariest part?
Their AI Forward Deployed Engineers (AI FDEs) recently migrated a legacy data warehouse in 5 days — a task that would traditionally take 2 years.
I've spent years optimizing pipelines, tuning Spark jobs, designing star schemas. And I genuinely believe the engineers who will win the next decade are the ones who stop thinking only in pipelines and start thinking in semantic data models and operational AI.

The question isn't "should we adopt Palantir?"

The real question is: Are you building data infrastructure or building data intelligence?

The gap between those two things is getting very expensive very fast.
What's your read — is Palantir's Ontology layer a genuine paradigm shift, or is it enterprise hype dressed in fancy terminology?

AI's getting the blame for broken software budgets, but that's only half the diagnosis.

Bain recently dropped a piece on the SaaS slowdown that's worth the read. 

The structural threat is real — installed bases are holding for now but investor confidence has shifted and the seat-based model is under genuine pressure. 

They put it plainly: a great product in a growing market used to compensate for weak operations. That's no longer true.

But from an operating seat, there's a layer underneath that the article doesn't fully address.

AI is exposing what was already broken.

For years, companies were buying software they didn't fully use and hiring roles they didn't fully need. 

The growth curve covered a lot of sins. 

Nobody defined the outcome before they signed the contract, measured whether adoption actually delivered value, or asked the hard question.

AI made that problem impossible to ignore.

Bain says the move now is to clarify your AI strategy and build fast. I agree completely. But you can't build on a foundation you never examined.

The companies that come out of this strongest won't just have a better AI roadmap. They'll have finally gotten honest about what they actually needed in the first place.

If you can't define the outcome before you sign the contract — don't sign it.

Your marketing team is working hard. Content is going out. Campaigns are running. Analytics are being tracked.

And your revenue is still flat. So you are thinking about hiring someone new, or switching agencies, or buying a better tool.

Do not do any of that yet.

More execution will not fix an ownership problem. And what most flat-growth companies have is not a talent problem or a budget problem. It is an accountability problem. Nobody in the building is actually responsible for whether marketing produces revenue. Someone manages the channels. Someone executes the plan. But nobody owns the outcome.

AI tools have made this worse. A single marketer can now produce more content, more campaigns, and more activity than ever before. If the strategy is missing or wrong, AI just scales the damage faster.

Before you hire, before you sign another contract, ask one question: who in your company is accountable for marketing growth, by name, with a defined revenue target attached to their role.

If you hesitate, that is your answer.

I wrote about this in detail, including what the fix actually looks like in practice: https://bit.ly/3OdUYlM 

Am I wrong? Tell me what you are seeing.

Imagine a champion of human writing/editing working for an AI company.
*record scratch*
Yep, that's me. You're probably wondering how I got here.

I like to think of myself as a mole or "man on the inside." But anyway ...

It all started in the dark days after losing my most recent 9-to-5.

An AI company that always seems to be hiring copy editors, writers, generalists, proofreaders, auditors, etc., posted a contract job for writing domain experts or something highfalutin like that.

Since I needed something, anything, I threw my hat in the ring. 
Fast forward a couple of months later: I get an email offering me the job.

Hesitantly, I go through the onboarding process, which is — unsurprisingly for an AI company — almost all automated.

The next thing I know, I'm writing and editing the responses AI gives users.

Yes, I'm technically playing on the wrong team, but if this is my way to make sure AI is accurate, reliable, and as close to human as it can get, then I'm calling it a win. 

Plus, I mean, yeah, there's the whole "I get paid" aspect too. Which is nice.

Honestly, it's a good supplement to my client work to have. It fills in the ebbs when client jobs aren't flowing.

Have I sold my soul? You tell me.
Do I feel guilty? Nope.
Is it honorable work? Is any paid work "honorable"?
Do I recommend this type of job? Sure, if you're OK with it.

What about you? Do you think less of me as a human advocate because of this?

💪🏼 As always, written by a human. 💪🏼

Harvey just announced a brand partnership with Gabriel Macht from Suits. Legora responded by hiring Jude Law. Legora is absolutely trolling Harvey.

Celebrity endorsements for legal operations software. That tells you where the marketing budget is going. And it is not into solving the workflow problems in-house teams actually face.

Here is the problem. Harvey, Legora, and the other big legal AI tools were built for law firms. Document review, contract analysis, research. They are good at that.

But that is a fraction of what General Counsel do.

Talk to any GC and they will tell you the bulk of their day is business strategy, stakeholder management, cross-functional decisions, and operational work that has nothing to do with legal tasks in the traditional sense. The legal AI tools were not designed for any of that. They automate legal work without understanding the business context those tasks live in.

And the pricing reflects who they were built for.

Vertical legal AI tools embed deep into law firm workflows and come with enterprise price tags that most in-house teams cannot justify. General AI tools offer comparable output at a fraction of the cost, and they adapt to the full scope of what GCs actually do.

Legal operations managers are using Claude to build their own lightweight tech solutions on top of the software they already buy, without procurement cycles or IT projects. GC teams that have deployed general-purpose AI are handling higher contract volumes in-house, which reduces external spend and builds internal credibility with the business.

The Word add-in is the latest piece of that. Claude for Word launched in public beta this week as a native sidebar add-in. Contract review, redline analysis, tracked changes, all inside the document environment lawyers already live in. No separate platform. No six-figure implementation. It also connects with Claude for Excel and PowerPoint, meaning a single conversation thread can span a contract, a financial model, and a board presentation simultaneously.

In-house teams will keep funding this. A $25 per seat subscription that covers the full scope of GC work is an easy budget conversation. A six-figure vertical tool that automates a narrow slice of it is not.

That is the real threat to Harvey, Legora, and every purpose-built legal AI platform. Not a competing law firm tool. Not a better contract review engine. It is a general AI that in-house teams are already paying for, already embedding into their workflows, and already preferring because it fits how they actually work, not how a law firm bills.

Legal AI built for law firms will survive as long as law firms keep paying for it. But the in-house market is going to make a different choice. And that choice may prove fatal for the vertical legal AI model.

Most solo attorneys think hiring a better associate will fix their capacity problem — and that belief is exactly why they stay buried.

The bottleneck isn't headcount. It's that every time you add a person, you add a new system of chaos they have to navigate just to do their job.

You're the one reviewing intake notes that weren't taken consistently. You're manually chasing down documents that should have been auto-requested on day one. Your paralegal is copy-pasting client info between three platforms that were never meant to talk to each other. Your follow-up sequence is whatever you remember to do on a Tuesday afternoon between two client calls. Contract review takes four hours because it always does — not because it has to. You onboard a new matter and the first thirty minutes are just orientation, not work.

When that operational weight compounds long enough, you stop taking cases you should take. You miss follow-ups on leads your marketing paid to generate. You start making decisions based on bandwidth, not strategy.

This is the Operator Trap — the firm's entire intelligence lives inside the founder, and every hire just creates more surface area for that intelligence to leak out of. It's not a people problem. It's an architecture problem.

The five AI capabilities that actually move the needle for a solo firm — intake automation, document drafting, client communication, matter tracking, and contract review — aren't about replacing your team. They're about making sure the firm works the same way whether you're in it or not.

A firm that runs on systems doesn't make you less essential — it makes you impossible to outwork.

Two areas that are only becoming more important.

He started a law firm in September 2020.

Two lawyers, a pandemic, and no guarantees.

Five years later, Joshua Strub and his partner have built one of Canada's most specialized construction law boutiques — 10 lawyers deep, handling the kind of complex infrastructure and transit disputes that most firms don't have the bench for.

But what makes Josh's perspective on AI particularly interesting isn't his enthusiasm for it. It's his caution.

"Whether we're endorsing it or not, it's happening. So we need to be ahead of it."

He's not afraid of hallucinations. He's more worried about what AI quietly misses — and he's got a document review story that illustrates exactly why that distinction matters.

In this episode of Main Street AI, Josh and I get into:

→ Why construction law is really about who pays when a project runs over time and over budget 
→ The square peg problem: why standard form contracts don't fit every situation 
→ Shadow IT in law firms — junior lawyers are using ChatGPT whether the firm signs off or not 
→ His real concern about AI and the next generation: what happens to lawyers who never had to develop their skills the hard way? 
→ The future scenario where parties agree to let AI resolve minor disputes instead of hiring lawyers 
→ How he's built a firm where a first-year gets the same opportunity as a seventh-year — and people actually want to stay

He also shares the magic wand answer that every litigator secretly thinks about: what it would mean to turn legal services into a recurring revenue model.

If you're in law, construction, or just thinking carefully about where AI earns trust and where it still has to prove itself — this one's worth your time.

The secret is in the hidden vocabulary in your reviews.

The Meta/Google algorithms no longer care about the interest boxes you check. The Creative is the Targeting.

If you use "Brand-Speak," you get broad, expensive traffic.

If you use the specific "Human-Speak" from your reviews, you target via psychology.

By highlighting specific frictions and outcomes from real customers, you remove the emotional risk of the purchase.

When your ads use the messy, irregular truth found in a 3-paragraph review, that effort creates an "Attention Debt" that the consumer pays with their time.
